special_medical_emergency_procedures.special_medical_emergency_procedures:
1. GENERAL APPROACH
All medical emergencies will be managed using the ICS framework with clear communication through the Medical Unit Leader (MEDL), Division/Group Supervisors, and Communications/Dispatch. Any responder injury or illness, no matter how minor, must be reported up the chain of command and logged with the Medical Unit at Base Camp. The priority is life safety, followed by incident stabilization and protection of property.

2. INITIAL ON-SCENE CARE
- First contact: The closest qualified responder (EMT/paramedic/first-aid trained firefighter) will provide immediate care using standard wildland firefighting medical kits and PPE.
- Scene safety: Before patient contact, responders will evaluate for falling snags, rolling debris, active fire, smoke conditions, and unstable footing. If the area is unsafe, the patient will be moved to the nearest safe area when feasible.
- Basic assessment: Perform primary survey (airway, breathing, circulation, disability, exposure) and control major bleeding immediately. Use tourniquets for life-threatening extremity hemorrhage and pressure dressings for moderate bleeding.
- Documentation: As soon as practical, complete a short field medical report including patient name (if known), assignment, division, nature of injury, vital signs, allergies, medications, and mechanism of injury.

3. NOTIFICATION & COMMUNICATION
- Notify chain of command: The first responder notifies Division/Group Supervisor, who then notifies Operations and the Medical Unit Leader via radio.
- Radio format: Use clear text on Command Med Net 155.340 MHz. Example: "Medical, this is Division Alpha with a Priority 1 medical. 35-year-old male firefighter, possible femur fracture, conscious and breathing, located at Division Alpha, near Drop Point 3. Requesting ground and potential air evacuation."
- Priority levels:
  • Priority 1 (Immediate): Life-threatening or potentially disabling injuries/illnesses (e.g., major trauma, chest pain, anaphylaxis, severe burns, altered mental status, heat stroke).
  • Priority 2 (Delayed): Serious but not immediately life-threatening conditions (e.g., stable fractures, moderate burns, controlled bleeding, moderate dehydration).
  • Priority 3 (Minor): Minor injuries/illnesses treatable at aid station or base (e.g., minor sprains, abrasions, mild smoke irritation, blisters).

4. PATIENT MOVEMENT FROM THE LINE
- Extraction point: Identify the nearest safe and accessible pickup point (road, dozer line, or helispot). Mark it with visible flagging and ensure access for engines/UTVs.
- Litter teams: Each division will maintain at least one designated litter team trained in low-angle evacuation using a Stokes basket or similar device.
- UTV/Type 6 transport: For moderate injuries where road access is available, use a staffed utility terrain vehicle (UTV) or Type 6 engine to move the patient to the Division Aid Station or designated ambulance transfer point.

5. HELICOPTER / AIR AMBULANCE USE
- Indications: Consider air evacuation for Priority 1 patients, especially in cases of major trauma, suspected spinal injuries, significant burns, or when ground transport time to a trauma/burn center exceeds 60 minutes.
- Coordination: All helicopter requests are coordinated through the Air Operations Branch and MEDL. Provide patient priority, location (Lat/Long or clear landmark), hazards (wires, trees, smoke), and LZ description.
- Landing zones (LZs): Use established helispots when possible. If creating a new LZ, ensure a minimum 100' x 100' clear area, slope <10 degrees, and free of loose debris. Assign an LZ manager with radio contact on Air-to-Ground 159.300 MHz.
- Packaging: Patient will be fully secured on a backboard or vacuum mattress, with C-spine precautions as indicated. Ensure all loose gear is secured before helicopter arrival.

6. HEAT ILLNESS & SMOKE EXPOSURE PROTOCOLS
- Heat cramps/exhaustion: Remove from heat, move to shade, loosen clothing, provide oral fluids (water and electrolyte solution), and cool with wet towels or misting fans at Rehab. Monitor vital signs and mental status.
- Heat stroke: Treat as Priority 1 emergency. Rapid cooling (ice packs to groin/axilla/neck, cool water immersion if feasible), immediate evacuation, and early notification to receiving hospital.
- Smoke inhalation: Remove from smoke, administer oxygen if available, and monitor for respiratory distress, confusion, or chest pain. Consider transport to Sutter Auburn Faith Hospital for evaluation.

7. BURNS
- Minor burns (superficial/partial thickness <5% BSA): Cool with clean water, cover with dry sterile dressings, manage pain, and evaluate at Base Camp Medical.
- Significant burns (partial/full thickness >5% BSA, face, hands, feet, groin, or circumferential burns): Treat as Priority 1. Cover with dry sterile sheets, avoid ice directly on tissue, maintain warmth, and arrange direct transport to UC Davis Medical Center Burn Center via air if possible.

8. TRAUMA, FALLS, AND CRUSH INJURIES
- Spinal precautions: For falls greater than 3–5 feet, or any high-energy mechanism, maintain C-spine precautions and immobilize as indicated.
- Crush injuries: Treat as Priority 1. Control bleeding, monitor for signs of shock, and minimize time under compression if safe to do so. Notify receiving facility of suspected crush syndrome so they can prepare appropriate interventions.

9. CONTAGIOUS ILLNESS & COVID-LIKE SYMPTOMS
- Screening: Anyone with fever, cough, sore throat, new loss of taste/smell, vomiting/diarrhea, or flu-like symptoms should report to Base Camp Medical for evaluation.
- Isolation: Symptomatic individuals will be masked, evaluated in a designated isolation area, and, if indicated, transported for testing. Housing and feeding units will be notified to arrange separate lodging if the individual remains on incident.
- Reporting: MEDL will track clusters of similar illness and coordinate with the Safety Officer and local public health authorities as appropriate.

10. MINOR INJURIES & RETURN-TO-WORK
- Blisters, minor strains, small cuts, eye irritation, and mild smoke exposure will generally be managed at Base Camp or Division Aid Stations.
- All treated individuals must check in with MEDL for documentation and determination of fitness for continued duty.
- Return-to-work decisions will be made by the Medical Unit in consultation with the Safety Officer and, when appropriate, the individual’s home agency.

11. DOCUMENTATION & WORKERS’ COMP
- Every injury or illness will have a medical encounter form completed and filed with the Medical Unit.
- For significant injuries requiring off-incident treatment, MEDL will assist the patient in initiating workers’ compensation or equivalent agency injury reports.
- Copies of all records will be provided to the Incident Commander’s staff as required and to the home agency at demobilization.

12. DEMOBILIZATION MEDICAL CHECKS
- Prior to demobilization, resources with known injuries or recent illness will receive a brief medical check.
- MEDL will provide recommendations for follow-up care and any work restrictions to the individual and their home agency representative.

These procedures are intended to be practical and field-focused, reflecting the realities of steep terrain, variable communications, and rapidly changing fire behavior in the Redwood Canyon Wildfire incident.
